correctAnswer: At rest and moving at constant velocity both have zero acceleration
acceptableAlternatives: acceleration is zero for uniform motion or rest
quizQuestion: What do 'at rest' and 'moving at constant velocity' have in common regarding acceleration?
explanation: Both states have zero acceleration (a = 0). 'At rest' is simply the special case where velocity = 0 and a = 0. 'Constant velocity' means v ≠ 0 but a = 0. Newton's First Law covers both: in either case, the net force must be zero. A common misconception is that 'at rest' and 'in motion' are fundamentally different — they are not; both require ΣF = 0.
visualDescription: Two scenarios side by side: a stationary car (a=0, v=0) and a car moving at constant highway speed (a=0, v=constant)
distractors: Both require a non-zero net force
Both require gravity to be zero
Both have the same acceleration (equal to g)
Both have a net force equal to weight
Both are examples of static equilibrium

correctAnswer: Negative acceleration means acceleration in the negative direction — it could be speeding up or slowing down
acceptableAlternatives: negative acceleration depends on the direction of velocity
quizQuestion: Does negative acceleration always mean an object is slowing down?
explanation: No. Negative acceleration means acceleration in the negative direction. If the object also moves in the negative direction (negative velocity), it SPEEDS UP. Only when velocity and acceleration have opposite signs does the object slow down. A ball thrown upward has downward (negative) acceleration throughout — it slows going up, stops, then speeds up going down — all with the same acceleration.
visualDescription: A ball thrown upward with a downward acceleration arrow constant throughout, showing slowing on the way up and speeding on the way down
distractors: Yes, negative acceleration always means slowing down
Negative acceleration means the object has stopped
Negative acceleration applies only to falling objects
Negative acceleration is the same as deceleration always
Negative acceleration means speed is decreasing
Negative acceleration means the object moves left
Negative acceleration only occurs in the vertical direction

correctAnswer: Straight line with constant slope (slope = velocity)
acceptableAlternatives: linear x-t graph means constant velocity
quizQuestion: What does the position-time (x-t) graph look like for an object moving at constant velocity?
explanation: Constant velocity → constant slope of x-t graph → straight line. The slope equals the velocity (v = Δx/Δt = rise/run). A steeper slope means faster constant motion. A horizontal line (slope = 0) means the object is at rest. A curved x-t graph indicates changing velocity (non-zero acceleration). Recognizing straight vs. curved x-t graphs is an essential AP skill.
visualDescription: A straight line on an x-t graph labeled 'constant slope = constant velocity'

acceptableAlternatives: curved x-t graph means changing velocity (acceleration present)
quizQuestion: What shape does the position-time (x-t) graph have for an object under constant acceleration?
explanation: x = x₀ + v₀t + ½at² is a quadratic in t → the x-t graph is parabolic. The curve opens upward for positive acceleration, downward for negative acceleration. The slope (velocity) changes continuously. Free-fall produces a downward-opening parabola for height vs. time (taking up as positive). Projectile motion height vs. time is also parabolic.
visualDescription: A parabolic x-t graph curving upward, labeled 'constant acceleration produces parabolic x-t graph'

acceptableAlternatives: SI unit of velocity is meters per second
quizQuestion: What are the SI units of velocity and speed?
explanation: Velocity and speed are both measured in m/s (meters per second) in SI. Acceleration: m/s². Displacement and distance: m. Time: s. Knowing SI units helps check equations by dimensional analysis: if you compute ΔKE = ½mv² and get kg × (m/s)² = kg⋅m²/s² = J, the units confirm the formula is correct (joules).
visualDescription: A speedometer labeled in m/s with the SI unit highlighted

correctAnswer: v = 0 at the peak; acceleration = g downward throughout
acceptableAlternatives: zero velocity at maximum height, g downward always
quizQuestion: At the peak of a vertically thrown ball's trajectory, what are its velocity and acceleration?
explanation: At the peak: velocity = 0 (momentarily stopped — direction changing from upward to downward). Acceleration = g ≈ 10 m/s² DOWNWARD (unchanged throughout the entire flight — gravity doesn't 'turn off' at the peak). This is a classic AP misconception: students often say 'acceleration is zero at the peak' because velocity is zero — WRONG. A = −g continuously.
visualDescription: Ball at peak height with v = 0 labeled and a downward acceleration arrow g labeled (common misconception 'a = 0 here?' crossed out)
distractors: v = 0 and a = 0 at the peak
v is maximum downward and a = g
v = g downward and a = g downward
v = 0 and a changes direction at peak
v = 0 only briefly but a switches to upward at peak

correctAnswer: Time of flight depends on vertical motion only (not horizontal velocity)
acceptableAlternatives: horizontal velocity doesn't affect how long projectile stays in air
quizQuestion: For a projectile launched horizontally from a cliff, what determines the time it takes to hit the ground?
explanation: Time of flight depends only on vertical motion: the height h of the cliff and g. From y = ½gt²: t = √(2h/g). The horizontal velocity has ZERO effect on time of flight — a ball thrown horizontally from a cliff hits the ground at the same time as one dropped from the same height. This demonstrates the independence of horizontal and vertical motion.
visualDescription: Two balls launched from a cliff — one dropped, one thrown horizontally — both hitting the ground simultaneously
distractors: Horizontal velocity determines time of flight
Both horizontal and vertical motion determine time of flight
Time of flight is always the same regardless of height
A faster horizontal launch takes longer to fall
Time depends on the launch angle only
Time of flight equals horizontal distance divided by speed
Time is determined by the angle between velocity and gravity

correctAnswer: The projectile returns to the same height with the same speed but opposite vertical velocity
acceptableAlternatives: launch speed equals landing speed for symmetric trajectory
quizQuestion: For a projectile launched and landing at the same height, how do the launch and landing speeds compare?
explanation: By conservation of energy (or symmetry of projectile motion), the speed at landing equals the speed at launch when the projectile returns to the same height. The horizontal velocity component is unchanged (v_x constant). The vertical component v_y is equal in magnitude but opposite in direction (up on launch, down on landing). Therefore: v_final = v_initial in magnitude.
visualDescription: Symmetric projectile arc showing equal speeds at launch and landing, with horizontal velocity constant and vertical velocity reversed
distractors: Landing speed is always greater due to gravity
Landing speed is less than launch speed
Speed at landing depends on horizontal distance traveled
The projectile speeds up on the way down so lands faster
Launch and landing speeds are only equal if launched at 45°
Landing speed equals launch speed only for vertical throws
